$comment: THE FACTS MANIFEST. Every client-visible fact has exactly ONE allowed producer path. Any other file that matches a fact's producer patterns is a SECOND DOOR and fails CI. The stale door is the one the client sees: this class shipped the ghost jurisdiction, the 'Sector regulator' label and the GBP 17.5M fine that never reached the client. Paths ending in '/' are prefixes: any file under them is allowed. Patterns are JavaScript regex source strings, applied per-file with the m flag. A fact tagged "kind": "producer-fn" carries FUNCTION-NAME patterns: in those patterns a (?<pname>...) named group anchors the producer NAME so check.js can exempt a CONSUMER member-call site (name preceded by '.'/'?.' AND followed by '(') while still flagging every definition, export and re-derivation. See tools/one-door/check.js for the exact two-part exemption rule. The DATA-LITERAL facts (fine, regulator, law-title, host, element-checklist) carry no pname group and fire on first match anywhere, unchanged.

facts.label: JURISDICTION NEXUS (which legal regimes bind the subject)
facts.note: Tier A register/authorisation/registered-office, Tier B office-postcode/local-phone/ccTLD, Tier C prose never attaches. The NEXUS RESOLUTION (attach rule, tier ladder, EU derivation) has exactly one door: facts/jurisdiction.js. facts/vocabulary.js is the shared vocabulary keystone and is a co-door ONLY for the jurisdiction VOCABULARY primitives it owns (the canonical code list, famCanon family canonicaliser, isJurisdiction validator) that every facts module imports rather than re-declares; it produces no nexus itself. catalogue/schema.js validates required_nexus enum membership against the vocabulary; it never derives or produces a nexus - production stays solely in facts/jurisdiction.js.

facts.label: HOST ANCHORING (which host the evidence and claims are anchored to)
facts.note: Every artifact and every claim is anchored to one canonical registrable host, computed in one place.

facts.label: SECTOR CLASSIFICATION (which sector/sub-sector the subject is)
facts.note: One canonical sector tree, one classifier door (LLM-gated with register cross-check lives behind it).

facts.label: REGULATOR NAME (the authority a client is told about)
facts.note: The catalogue is the ONLY source of regulator names. A regulator string literal anywhere else is a second door.

facts.label: FINE AMOUNT (the figure a client is quoted)
facts.note: Fines come from the catalogue's penalty field only. A hard-coded fine in code fails CI: this exact class shipped a wrong GBP 17.5M figure.

facts.label: LAW / STATUTE TITLE (the name of the law a client is shown)
facts.note: Never render a law without provenance. A statute title as a string literal outside the catalogue is a fabrication vector.

facts.label: ELEMENT CHECKLIST (the per-rule required elements and their evaluation)
facts.note: Element checklists are catalogue data. Evaluation consumes the compiled catalogue artifact; it never declares elements of its own.

facts.label: IDENTITY (legal name, company number, registered office)
facts.note: Register-first ladder (schema.org legalName/sameAs, og:site_name, register match, title, domain stem) lives in one file and emits legal_name + company_number + registered_office.

facts.label: CAPABILITY PREDICATES (which activities the subject does: ecommerce, ads, AI, payments, UGC, ...)
facts.note: Capability predicates gate which frameworks apply (the ICP served-cells doctrine downstream). One derivation door: each predicate is present / absent / unknown with evidence, and absence is only asserted once the page budget is met. A second capability classifier anywhere else is a second door.
